“Based on where we are, moving much faster, much bigger in the direction of using much more geothermal, frankly, is all good news,” Wagner says.
To go faster and deeper will require advances in drilling technologies.
Companies are developing drilling equipment that is more stable when breaking through hard rock at high temperatures.
Some firms are even aiming to penetrate rock without using standard drills.
Quaise, a company with roots at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), is using a technology called millimetre wave drilling. The frequency is similar to that of microwaves.
Quaise’s application involves “sending electromagnetic waves in the microwave millimetre wave spectrum to essentially melt and vaporise through the rock,” explains Harry Kelso, Quaise’s communications manager.
Traditional geothermal energy clusters around hotspots on the earth’s surface where very hot rocks can be easily accessed.
“Millimetre wave drilling really enables you to access super-hot geothermal just about anywhere in the world,” says Kelso.
While Quaise is planning to use some conventional drilling at the project site it’s developing in Oregon, Kelso says that conventional drills start to break down more quickly when it reaches very hard rock.
Replacing drill bits increases the cost and time of drilling.
In Quaise’s case, Kelso says, “millimetre wave drilling is really what changes that because we’re not using a physical drill bit.”
Other companies are also working on advanced drilling technology, such as projectiles that move several times faster than the speed of sound.
Another crucial resource in the process is water. While some types of next-generation geothermal could create risks of water contamination or overconsumption, careful design can avoid this problem.
Initially Quaise’s system requires a lot of water, but according to Kelso, once the water is in the system it is continually circulated over the super-hot rocks.
“We’re essentially just recycling the water over and over,” he says.
Quaise is continuing to raise funds, with the aim of its Oregon project being up and running by 2030.
Like other early versions of geothermal systems, it’s an expensive project to get up and running.
“The economics are somewhat challenging,” Kelso admits. “Geothermal today is still more expensive because you are not getting as much power out of the well as you would if you were using that well for fossil fuel.”
But Quaise hopes that by targeting very high temperatures, of between 300C and 500C, the economics will improve.
While the higher end of that temperature range is ambitious, it’s a case of the-hotter-the-better.
“It allows you to get 10 times more energy per well from geothermal, which changes the economics and the power potential of geothermal,” according to Kelso.
In May, the Texas company Fervo Energy generated huge interest by becoming the first next-generation geothermal company to become publicly traded. It was initially valued at around $7.7bn. (BBC)
At the time of writing, shares are up around 18% from their IPO price.
Fervo quotes a construction cost for its Utah plant of $7,000 per kilowatt of electricity, which it says is comparable to traditional nuclear power.
And while that’s expensive, Fervo points out that, like other renewable energy sources, it does not have any ongoing costs for fuel.
“Over time, our goal is to achieve scale and drive down prices such that we’re able to outcompete gas,” the company said in its IPO filing.
Fervo has one high-profile customer – in 2021 it signed a deal sell its energy to Google, which needs vast amounts of electricity of its new datacentres.
It also has backing from Breakthrough Energy, a venture by Microsoft founder Bill Gates to accelerate innovative electricity production. (BBC)

title: filmmaker, actor Bhagyaraj dies at 73
short_intro: Legendary Tamil filmmaker and actor K Bhagyaraj, known for his subtle humour and witty dialogues, well connecting with the audience, died on Saturday following a cardiac arrest, a source in the hospital where he was admitted said.He was 73.His roles, mostly drawn from the middle class, held a charm as his exquisite screenplay combined with
content:
Legendary Tamil filmmaker and actor K Bhagyaraj, known for his subtle humour and witty dialogues, well connecting with the audience, died on Saturday following a cardiac arrest, a source in the hospital where he was admitted said.He was 73.
His roles, mostly drawn from the middle class, held a charm as his exquisite screenplay combined with his on-screen persona. In a career spanning over five decades, Bhagyaraj gave numerous hits, and some of his celebrated directorial and action works include – Andha 7 naatkal, Mundhanai Mudichu, Chinna Veedu, and Enga Chinna Rasa.
He is survived by his former actress wife Poornima, and their children, actor Shanthanu and Saranya. Apart from his film career, Bhagyaraj has been the editor of the Tamil weekly magazine Bhagya, and he has authored several novels.
His demise marks the end of an era in Tamil cinema, especially his exceptional storytelling and screenplay abilities. (PTI)
Born as Krishnaswamy Bhagyaraj in Tamil Nadu’s Erode district, he commenced his career as an assistant to legendary filmmaker Bharathiraja (who died recently) and later emerged as a successful director with films such as Suvarilladha Chiththirangal, Darling Darling Darling and Idhu Namma Aalu. He acted in over 75 films, apart from directing about 25 films.
Expressing shocked anguish over the death of Bhagyaraj, AIADMK General Secretary Edappadi K Palaniswami described him as a multi-talented film director, screenwriter, actor and music composer.
“Bhagyaraj was a great admirer of M G Ramachandran (AIADMK founder and former Chief Minister). He adapted the scenes from the late leader’s unfinished film ‘Anna Nee En Deivam’ (in 1977) and made a new screenplay based on it, making it a successful film called ‘Avasara Police 100’ (in1990),” Palaniswami said in a statement posted on ‘X’.

title: Chinese PLA encroaching on Indian territory near Taksing, claims group
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Chinese-PLA-encroaching-on-Indian-territory-near-Taksing-claims-group-696x313.webp

[ Bengia Ajum ]

TAKSING, 27 Jun:
 The Nah Welfare Society (NWS) has made a startling claim, alleging that the Chinese PLA is encroaching on Indian territory near Taksing in Upper Subansiri district.

In a letter addressed to the deputy commissioner of the district, the NWS claimed that the PLA is expanding its presence by occupying new and strategically important areas of Indian territory along the border.

“They have constructed roads, bridges, and set up military camps at many locations along the border in our ancestral lands, which were our hunting grounds where we freely roamed and collected forest produce just a few years ago. Our cattle grazing areas are now under the occupation of the Chinese PLA,” the society alleged.

The NWS further claimed that PLA activities along the border areas of Taksing circle have increased manifold over the last 10 to 15 years, “with the intention of occupying as much Indian territory as possible.”

The society further alleged that areas such as Oying (2445) in the Asaphila area, Paniar (Chujarta area), Marpan (Marnafe), Portrang (Lake), and Tingdingtang (TG) – all located close to Taksing – have been captured by the PLA and they have set up a military camp.

 It claimed that some of these areas were under Indian control before 2020.

The NWS has urged the deputy commissioner to take up the matter with the state government and the Government of India.

“The intention behind and the pace of the present activities of the Chinese PLA in the Taksing area are very alarming and a matter of grave concern for us. We are losing our land inch by inch every day. On behalf of the entire people of Taksing circle, we request the government to immediately take necessary steps to safeguard our land from the Chinese PLA,” the society added.

The deputy commissioner did not respond to queries sent via WhatsApp.

title: Flood toll rises to two; IMD warns of more heavy rain
content:
ITANAGAR, 27 Jun: The toll in the heavy rain-triggered flash floods in Keyi Panyor district rose to two on Saturday after the body of another woman was retrieved from under the debris in the NEEPCO Colony, an official said.

The deceased has been identified as 30-year-old Ballari Marak alias Olyn Synkly of Meghalaya’s Ri-Bhoi district, Keyi Panyor District Disaster Management Officer (DDMO) Beru Dulam said.

Marak was among five persons who went missing after a flash flood, triggered by a cloudburst and incessant rainfall, struck the district on Wednesday morning.

With the latest recovery, two people have been confirmed dead, while three others remain missing. The body of a 35-year-old woman was found on Wednesday.

Search operations entered the fourth day on Saturday, with rescue teams launching raft operations from the dam side of the Panyor Lower Hydroelectric Project  to trace the missing persons swept away from the NEEPCO Colony.

State Disaster Management Secretary Dani Sulu said the operation is being carried out jointly by personnel of the National Disaster Response Force (NDRF), State Disaster Response Force (SDRF), district police and local volunteers.

To strengthen the operation, an Indian Air Force helicopter and a state civil aviation helicopter have been deployed to airlift additional SDRF personnel and rescue equipment to the affected area.

The flash flood devastated the NEEPCO Colony in Poosa, damaging houses, disrupting road connectivity and triggering landslides at several locations.

According to preliminary estimates, around 30 houses were completely damaged or washed away at the NEEPCO Colony, while another 10 houses were destroyed and 14 partially damaged in the Possa and Pitapool areas. A total of 128 households have been affected in the district. A relief camp has been set up on the NEEPCO campus, where 60 displaced people are currently taking shelter.

Meanwhile, restoration of road connectivity is progressing in Keyi Panyor and Papum Pare districts. The Potin-Kimin road has been reopened for light vehicles, while the Hoj-Sagalee stretch has also been restored, officials said.

The Yachuli-Pistana-Ambam-Selsango-Parang-Sagalee-Hoj-Itanagar route has likewise been opened for light vehicular traffic.

The India Meteorological Department (IMD) also warned of heavy to extremely heavy rainfall across several parts of the state from Sunday.

 A ‘red’ warning (take action) has been issued for parts of Lower Siang, West Siang and East Siang districts on Sunday, while ‘orange’ alert (be prepared) has been sounded for several adjoining districts.

Heavy to very heavy rainfall is expected to continue over parts of central and eastern Arunachal on Monday, with ‘orange’ warning in place for Lower Dibang Valley and adjoining areas.

Although rainfall intensity is likely to decrease from Tuesday, isolated heavy rain and thunderstorms are expected to persist across several districts before conditions improve further on Wednesday and Thursday.

The weather office has advised people living in vulnerable areas to remain alert for landslides, flash floods and waterlogging during the forecast period.(PTI)

title: Guv concerned over low rate of prosecution in POCSO cases
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Guv-concerned-over-696x249.webp

ITANAGAR, 27 Jun:
 Governor KT Parnaik expressed concern over the low rate of prosecution in POCSO cases in the state and called for a “whole of state” approach to protect women and children.

Chairing a high-level review meeting at the Lok Bhavan here on Saturday, the governor advised strengthening the Directorate of Prosecution to expedite delivery of justice.

During the meeting, Parnaik reviewed the progress of investigations and prosecutions, and the measures undertaken by the state police for the safety and protection of women and children.

Expressing deep concern over the rising crimes against women and children, particularly cases registered under the POCSO Act, Parnaik said such crimes not only inflict lifelong trauma on innocent children and families but also shake the conscience of society.

“Every case represents a child whose trust, safety, and future have been compromised, making it imperative for all stakeholders to act with urgency and commitment,” the governor said.

Stating that the responsibility of protecting women and children cannot rest solely with law enforcement agencies, he sought active participation from all sections of society, including gaon buras, community leaders, educational institutions, parents, and civil society organisations.

At the district level, he said, the DCs and SPs must take up the issue in a mission mode and ensure coordinated efforts.

“Even a small sense of responsibility and vigilance within communities can bring about a significant positive change,” he observed.

DIG (Crime) Dr Joy Tirkey provided a detailed presentation on crimes against women and children in the state.

He also briefed the governor on ‘Project Abhaya – A Strategic Police-Public Trust, Awareness and Community Safety Initiative’, a community-oriented programme of the Arunachal Pradesh Police aimed at strengthening public awareness, building trust between citizens and law enforcement agencies, and enhancing community participation in ensuring the safety and well-being of women and children.

Home Principal Secretary Kaling Tayeng, DGP Dr SD Singh Jamwal, Women and Child Development Commissioner Nyali Ete, Prosecution Secretary Nani Grayu and AIGP S Singpho participated in the deliberations and discussed measures to strengthen prevention, investigation, prosecution, and victim support mechanisms. (Lok Bhavan)

title: Intense rain, not reservoir, caused flash floods, clarifies NEEPCO
content:
ITANAGAR, 27 Jun: The North Eastern Electric Power Corporation (NEEPCO) Ltd clarified that the 24 June flash flood in the residential colony of the Panyor Lower hydropower station in Keyi Panyor district was triggered by a localised intense rainfall and was not related to the operation of the reservoir and the power house of the project.

According to the NEEPCO, the incident commenced at around 3 am after heavy rainfall over a small catchment in and around the project area. The rainfall caused two small streams near the administrative and dam site colony to overflow, which washed away 24 residential buildings and damaged several others, it said.

“As per the preliminary assessment, the sequence of events shows that the incident was due to localised heavy rainfall and not because of any sudden increase in flow from upstream of the river,” the NEEPCO stated in a release.

Further, the early warning system established for rainfall and reservoir inflow forecasting for the project did not record any high rainfall or high discharge during the period of the incident, it said.

“The incident was in no way related to the operation of reservoir and power house of the project. No impact has been reported in the downstream areas of the project as a result of this incident,” the release added.

title: Wangsu calls for strict enforcement of LM Acts and Rules
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Wangsu-calls-for-strict-enforcement-of-LM-Acts-and-Rules-696x489.webp

NAHARLAGUN, 27 Jun:
 Legal Metrology & Consumer Affairs Minister Gabriel D Wangsu called upon all enforcement officers of the department to ensure strict enforcement of the provisions of the Legal Metrology Acts & Rules in each district of the state, and to “expedite the enforcement works by mobile inspection team at zonal level.”

Addressing officials at a review meeting of the Legal Metrology & Consumer Affairs (LM&CA) Department held at the conference hall of the LM&CA controller here on Friday, Wangsu emphasised on “evolution of means and methodologies to equip all the field officers to deal effectively with the continuous changing dynamics of the business communities in the market.”

He advised imparting training to the enforcement officers by chalking out programmes to visit other states to adopt their best practices. He also advised all officers to expeditiously deal with and dispose of cases related to establishment works and enforcement activities.

Deliberating on the functioning of the department, LM&CA Secretary Mige Kamki asked all the officers to train and prepare themselves with all the office applications to become self-reliant in office works. He also advised the district level officials to work in a cohesive manner “for best results of the department by considering the annual revenue target as fixed by the state government, as the same shall be reflected upon APAR of the officer.”

LM&CA Controller Minte Siga exhorted all the members present to actively participate in the review meeting to draw a roadmap for the welfare of the department. He stressed on creating a peaceful and friendly atmosphere in the office for greater welfare of the department in the times ahead.

Assistant Controller (HQ) Gamkhong Singpho also spoke.

LM&CA Under secretary Kipa Yakum and all deputy controllers, assistant controllers, inspectors and other officer of the LM&CA Department were present at the review meeting.

title: POCSO case registered against anti-drug activist Gumin Mize
content:
Staff Reporter

ITANAGAR, 27 Jun:
 A fresh case, under the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, has been registered against “self-proclaimed” anti-drug activist Gumin Mize after a minor girl alleged that he attempted to sexually assault her, police said on Saturday.

According to the police, a written complaint was received at the women police station (WPS), Itanagar, on 20 June, alleging molestation of a minor girl by Mize.

Based on the complaint, the Itanagar women police station registered a case under Sections 74, 75(2), 79, 127 and 351 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), read with Sections 8 and 12 of the POCSO Act, and launched an investigation.

The police said that, as per the complaint and the preliminary investigation, “Mize, along with two associates linked to a self-proclaimed anti-drug group, allegedly took the victim with them, following which the accused allegedly attempted to force himself upon her.”

The victim, however, managed to escape from the situation and returned home, the complaint stated.

Investigators said the survivor’s statement has been recorded before both the police and the jurisdictional magistrate under the relevant legal provisions. According to the police, the statement made before the court corroborates the allegations contained in the complaint.

“The survivor’s statement has already been recorded before the police, as well as the court. After obtaining a production warrant to take the alleged accused into custody, the police will continue the investigation in the newly registered POCSO case,” police sources said.

Officials said further details are being withheld as the case involves a minor and the investigation is in progress. They added that all further legal action would be taken strictly in accordance with the law.

The latest case comes days after Mize was arrested in connection with another case registered at the women police station in Pasighat.

In that case, an FIR was lodged on 14 June under Sections 64 (1) (rape), 75 (2) (sexual harassment), 126 (2) (wrongful confinement) and 351 (3) (criminal intimidation) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, along with Section 27 of the Arms Act.

Mize was arrested on 18 June and was subsequently remanded to 14 days in judicial custody.

His bail application was rejected by the competent court on 25 June. Police said they would seek a production warrant to formally take him into custody in connection with the newly registered POCSO case as the investigation progresses.

title: Arunachal’s Laki Weshi Sangno creates history with pro MMA win in Indonesia
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Arunachals-Laki-Weshi-Sangno-creates-history-with-pro-MMA-win-in-Indonesia.webp

ITANAGAR, 27 Jun:
 Laki Weshi Sangno (professional cage name Wayshi Sangno) of East Kameng district has become the first fighter from Arunachal Pradesh to win a professional MMA bout in Indonesia after defeating Indonesia’s Namr Arad by first-round technical knockout (TKO) in the strawweight division at Canggu Fight Night 39.

Fighting for India, Sangno took control of the bout from the start. He picked up Arad, slammed him to the canvas and followed it up with ground-and-pound strikes.

Arad tried to fight back with two guillotine choke attempts from the bottom, but Sangno escaped both without much trouble. The Abrasumante Gym fighter continued landing punches from the top position, leaving the Indonesian with little chance to recover.

With about 30 seconds remaining in the opening round, the referee stopped the fight to save Arad from further punishment and declared Sangno the winner by TKO.

The victory is a major milestone for Arunachal’s MMA community, as Sangno became the first Arunachali to register a professional MMA victory in Indonesia.

Sangno is the son of Nani Weshi Sangno and late Darmang Weshi Sangno of Weshi village in Pakoti circle of East Kameng district.

title: Workshop on development of Nocte script held
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Workshop-on-development-of-Nocte-script-held-696x291.webp

KHONSA, 27 Jun:
 The Nocte Script Development Committee (NSDC), in collaboration with the Centre for Endangered Languages (CFEL), Arunachal Institute of Tribal Studies (AITS), Rajiv Gandhi University (RGU), organised a workshop on the development of the Nocte script at the deputy commissioner’s conference hall here in Tirap district on Saturday.

RGU linguist and resource person Dr Wanglit Mongchan guided deliberations on the adaptation of the Roman script for the Nocte language.

A drafting committee, comprising Gankhu Sumnyan, Noklam Bisai and Wangtum Homcha Lowang, was constituted to compile and document the deliberations and recommendations during the workshop.

Stakeholders from various Nocte villages, teachers from colleges and schools, scholars, and local leaders actively participated in the discussions. They empha-sised the importance of having a common Nocte script for cultural preservation and educational advancement.

It is the first step in a series of workshops planned for the future, aimed at further strengthening the foundation of the Nocte script and ensuring its wider acceptance across dialectal groups.

The workshop was chaired by Deputy Commissioner Techu Aran and attended by, among others, ZPC John KK Matey, ZPMs Najen Rangwang and Wanglem Thingnok, DoTCL DD Wangton Lowang, and other panchayat members from the community. (DIPRO)

title: Keyi Panyor police, locals brave storm for Yazali
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Keyi-Panyor-police-locals-brave-storm-for-Yazali-696x407.webp

YAZALI, 27 Jun:
 When the skies opened up over Keyi Panyor, triggering sudden flash floods and treacherous landslides, the local police force didn’t hesitate. They stepped directly into the storm to protect their community.

Guided by an emergency mobilisation order from SP Angad Mehta, these dedicated officers have been working continuous, gruelling shifts to manage dangerous search and rescue operations and keep vital transit lines open. Yet, the strength of a community is truly measured when its people stand together, and the rescue teams have found an invaluable ally in Bath David, a local youth who has voluntarily joined the frontlines to assist the team in their tireless efforts.

While relentless weather conditions continue to test the region, these quiet heroes, bolstered by the brave spirit of local citizens, are working round the clock to bring order to the chaos, support disaster response teams, and keep families safe.

Working shoulder-to-shoulder with the State Disaster Response Force (SDRF), localised search and rescue teams led by Toko Okey of the Yazali police station and Toko Tapa of the Yachuli police station are navigating hazardous conditions on the ground at the flood-hit NEEPCO Colony in Yazali.

At the same time, the battle to clear massive landslide debris along the critical Pitapool-Potin highway continues without pause. Standing in the mud and rain, traffic and naka duty teams are anchored by officers like Putul Hussain and Khoda Adilare, managing traffic at key points like Possa. Alongside these teams, the hands-on support of local people exemplifies the profound resilience of the community, ensuring that heavy machinery can clear roads safely, emergency vehicles can pass without delay, and stranded drivers remain organised and safe.

Reflecting on the relentless efforts of the force and the heartening support from local citizens, DSP (HQ) Hage Nado said that the personnel are operating under incredibly challenging conditions to ensure public safety and keep rescue pipelines moving. He emphasised that, through carefully coordinated shifts and close collaboration with both the SDRF and community volunteers, the team’s core mission hasn’t changed since day one: protecting lives, clearing the way forward, and standing firmly by the people of Yazali and Yachuli.

The district administration and the local police have appealed to the public to support these officers and volunteers on the ground by cooperating with traffic diversions and always giving immediate right-of-way to emergency vehicles.

title: Int’l Day Against Drug Abuse observed with marathon
content:
AALO, 27 Jun: Marking the International Day Against Drug Abuse and Illicit Trafficking, the Aalo Miles Running Club organised a 5-km awareness run here in West Siang district on Thursday, promoting a drug-free society through fitness and healthy living.

The event was held in collaboration with the ICDS Department and the district medical office (DMO), and saw enthusiastic participation of youths and fitness enthusiasts across Aalo.

Speaking on the occasion, founders Yisum Bagra, Mini Ete, and Yasang Chije said the club aims to encourage healthy living through regular physical activity. They noted that Aalo Miles Running Club has been organising weekly Sunday runs and has now completed its 11th consecutive week, steadily building a culture of fitness and community participation.

The organisers thanked the ICDS Department and the DMO office for their support and reaffirmed their commitment to promoting fitness and drug awareness through similar initiatives. (DIPRO)

title: Science centre hosts aviation outreach prog
content:
ITANAGAR, 27 Jun: The Arunachal Pradesh Science Centre, a unit of the AP State Council for Science & Technology (APSCS&T), in collaboration with New Delhi-based Centre for Scientific Outreach, hosted an aviation outreach programme here on Saturday.

The initiative aimed to familiarise students with emerging developments in aviation, aerospace, and drone technology while encouraging them to explore future career opportunities. The event saw the participation of approximately 100 attendees, including students from Green Mount School and various other schools across the ICR, accompanied by escort teachers, 1st AP Bn NCC cadets from Rajiv Gandhi Government Polytechnic College, and internship trainees from Dera Natung Government College.

Addressing the gathering, APSCS&T Chairman Dr Tage Taki urged the students to maximise their use of available scientific infrastructure. Emphasising the transformative potential of unmanned aerial vehicles, he highlighted how drones can revolutionise the transportation of life-saving medicines and essential supplies to remote, inaccessible areas of the state.

Dr Taki encouraged the students to develop a solution-oriented mindset to address real-world challenges. He advised them to utilise the reverse engineering facility at the Centre of Excellence for Science Technology Innovation and Research, reminding them that “young minds are like a blank canvas,” and stressing the importance of lifelong curiosity.

AP Science Centre curator Vivek Kumar emphasised that scientific outreach must extend beyond classrooms to the grassroots level. He noted that in a geographically challenging state like Arunachal Pradesh, drone technology is vital for improving connectivity, development, and public service delivery.

The programme featured a blend of theoretical insights and hands-on learning, led by industry experts.

National Aviation Olympiad (Centre for Scientific Outreach, New Delhi) national coordinator Vaibhav Varun conducted an insightful session on emerging technologies in India. He guided the students through drone regulations, certification pathways, aviation safety, global employment prospects, and career opportunities.

A live demonstration was conducted at the parade ground, IG Park, by Gajendra Kumar Gangwar, an expert from the North East Centre for Technology Application and Reach, Guwahati. The participants were introduced to UAV fundamentals, DGCA Drone Rules, 2021, sensor payloads, calibration procedures, safety protocols, and flight simulations.

The comprehensive outreach programme provided the participants with vital insights into contemporary aviation developments, bridging the gap between theoretical science and practical, modern-day technological applications.

title: Retd industry director Goken Basar passes away
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Retd-industry-director-Goken-Basar-passes-away.webp

BASAR, 27 Jun:
 Goken Basar, a pioneering public servant, breathed his last on 26 June after a prolonged illness.

Born in 1950 in Gori village in Leparada district, Basar was also a distinguished figure of his community who dedicated his life to serving the people.

He graduated from JN College, Pasighat, in 1972, and earned his Master of Arts (MA) degree from Gauhati University in 1974. The achievement made him the first from the Basar clan to earn an MA degree, setting a path of inspiration for generations to come.

Basar’s professional life was defined by an unwavering commitment to the growth and administration of the region.

He began his career in the Education Department as a junior teacher at the Desi Lower Primary School and the Basar High School in 1974 and 1975.

Recognising his leadership potential, he was appointed as a circle officer in 1975, beginning an illustrious career that saw him serving in critical roles across the state.

He served as a circle officer and block development officer in Nari and Diyun/Miao, an assistant project officer and block development officer in Aalo, and an extra assistant officer in Likabali. His reputation for dedicated service grew as he took on the roles of extra assistant officer and block development officer in Jung and Basar, and served as a subdivisional officer in Namsai and Tuting.

As the first additional deputy commissioner in Tuting and later as a deputy secretary for DA/GA and transport in Itanagar, his influence and guidance were felt across various levels of government.

He later served as deputy commissioner of Upper Subansiri and retired as director of the Department of Industry in 2008.

Beyond his official roles, Basar will be remembered as a trailblazing icon of the community, having been the first from his clan to hold the positions of circle officer, deputy commissioner, and director of the Department of Industry.

title: Apa urges youths to work hard to achieve success
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Apa-urges-youths-to-work-hard-to-achieve-success.webp

Meritorious students felicitated

ITANAGAR, 27 Jun:
 Retired senior police officer Chukhu Apa has urged youths not to lose focus and to work hard towards achieving success in life.

Apa, who recently retired as inspector general of police (IGP), made the appeal while attending the Academic and Professional Achievers Award Ceremony organised by the Bengia Welfare Society (BWS) here on Saturday. Citing his own life journey, he encouraged young people to remain focused on their goals.

“My journey started from the remote Kebi village in Keyi Panyor district. From Kebi to working in the Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India, and later serving in places like Kabul, Afghanistan, was possible only because of my hard work and education. Never lose sight of your goals, and keep dreaming of achieving greater heights in life,” said Apa.

Renowned educationist and director of Sarvasya IAS Academy, Rajiv Singh, who attended the event as the special invitee, spoke in detail about the prospects in the civil services and the importance of integrity in pursuing such a career.

“If you are not sincere during your preparation for the civil services, you will not crack the examination. Even after entering the service, if you do not uphold integrity, your career will be at risk. Integrity is an integral part of life,” said Singh.

He also urged parents not to impose their career choices on their children.

“Let children decide what is best for them. Extend your full support, so that they can achieve their dreams. Give them wings to fly,” he added.

Earlier, BWS chairman Bengia Tabb in his address said that the society’s primary focus is on career development and academic excellence of members of the Bengia clan.

“We want to focus on career building and academic excellence. We want to ensure that members of our clan continue to shine in every field,” said Tabb.

He also stated that the Bengia Welfare Society would always play a constructive role in society-building and would never take any step that could hurt any other group or clan.

On the occasion, clan members who earned promotions in their services, political achievers, and meritorious students were felicitated. Among the Class 10 students, Bengia Heiuntsang, Bengia Nite, Bengia Upai, Hanu Bengia, Bengia Naku, Bengia Oka, and Bengia Gungma were felicitated.

In Class 12 (Science), Bengia Jinam, Bengia Tachung, Bengia Asmin, Bengia Chandu, and Bengia Mama were felicitated.

In Class 12 (Arts), Bengia Kamyar, Bengia Metung, Bengia Meme, Bengia Bima, Bengia Tayong, Bengia Mamu, and Bengia Harming were felicitated.

In Class 12 (Commerce), Bengia Taram, Bengia Api, and Bengia Nana were felicitated.

title: Students sensitised to ill effects of online gaming
content:
PASIGHAT, 27 Jun: Students were sensitised to the ill effects of online gaming during an awareness programme held under ‘Sahakarita Utsav 2026’ at the GHSS in Borguli in East Siang district on Saturday.

Mebo BMMU cluster coordinator Tokmem Moyong conducted a session on the harmful effects of excessive online gaming, including addiction, poor academic performance, mental health issues, physical inactivity, and social isolation. The session concluded with an interactive question-and-answer segment.

District Cooperative Union chairman Malo Komut and vice chairman Lottek Pertin advised the students to use digital technology responsibly and balance online activities with academics, physical health and social life.

The students were also briefed on the Government of India’s advisory on online gaming and the importance of responsible digital use.

The programme concluded with a pledge against harmful online gaming, followed by a brief address by the school’s Principal Tani Pada.

The programme was organised by the assistant registrar of cooperative societies, Pasighat, and the Arunachal State Rural Livelihoods Mission, Mebo, in collaboration with the Monggu Ane Didum Multipurpose Cooperative Society Ltd. (DIPRO)

title: Arunachal profs attend IASE conference in Thimphu
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Arunachal-profs-attend-IASE-conference-in-Thimphu-696x573.webp

ITANAGAR, 27 Jun:
 Rajiv Gandhi University (RGU) Education Department Professor Anga Paduand Binny Yanga Government Women’s College Associate Professor Dr Minu Sono attended the 19th biennial conference of the International Association of Special Education (IASE) at the Royal Thimphu College in Thimphu, Bhutan, from 21-26 June.

The conference marked the 40th anniversary of the IASE and brought together academicians, practitioners, healthcare providers, social workers, clinicians, policymakers, psychologists, family members, and administrators from around the world to discuss innovations in special education for cultivating inclusive futures.

Delegates from 28 countries shared their experiences and models of inclusive education. The conference provided a platform for academic exchange, as well as cultural interaction. Participants also gained insights into Bhutan’s inclusive education system and its curriculum and practices, while experiencing the country’s traditional cuisine, culture, and attire.

During the event, Prof Padu delivered a presentation titled ‘Inclusive Practices at Elementary Stage in Arunachal Pradesh’. In her presentation, Prof Padu highlighted nine inclusive practices adopted by elementary school teachers in the state. She emphasised that small yet meaningful changes in teachers’ day-to-day classroom and school practices can significantly promote inclusion and support the holistic development of all learners.

The identified inclusive practices include: assigning students the responsibility of leading the national anthem, the daily pledge, news reading, and the ‘thought for the day’ during the morning assembly; adopting mixed-gender seating arrangements in classrooms; directing questions openly and providing equal opportunities for all students to respond, with particular encouragement to backbenchers during and at the end of lessons; appointing class captains based on students’ leadership qualities, academic sincerity, regular attendance, and obedience, rather than their socioeconomic background or intelligence; organising remedial classes and peer tutoring; engaging students in school kitchen gardening activities; encouraging students to wear traditional attire on the 15th of every month; and providing counselling on academic as well as personal issues.

Prof Padu concluded her paper by stating that these simple yet effective practices contribute to creating an inclusive learning environment that enables the full potential development of all elementary students, including those with disabilities and those from diverse socioeconomic, cultural, religious, and regional backgrounds.

Dr Sono presented a paper titled ‘Attitude of College Teachers towards Inclusive Education in Arunachal Pradesh’. She dwelt on the awareness and attitudes of college teachers towards inclusive education, and identified factors contributing to their “moderately positive attitude despite their awareness.” She concluded by emphasising the need for collective efforts to strengthen positive attitude towards inclusive education, noting that teachers play a crucial role in building an inclusive society.

Founded in 1985, the IASE is a non-profit organisation dedicated to improving the quality of life and service delivery for individuals with special needs worldwide. The association works with the vision of promoting inclusive and equitable education and support services for all individuals with special needs.

title: NCC CATC underway at RGGPC
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/NCC-CATC-underway-at-RGGPC-696x398.webp

ITANAGAR, 27 Jun:
 The 1 Arunachal Pradesh Battalion National Cadet Corps (1 AP Bn NCC) is conducting its final combined annual training camp (CATC-68) for the training year 2026-27 from 24 June to 3 July at Rajiv Gandhi Government Polytechnic College (RGGPC) here.

A total of 247 NCC cadets are participating in the camp under the leadership of the 1 AP Battalion NCC commanding officer. The camp is being conducted with the support of 10 military staff, three associate NCC officers (ANOs), and one caretaker officer (CTO), who are overseeing the training and welfare of the cadets.

Brigadier Mayank Vaid, the newly appointed commander of NCC group headquarters, Tezpur also attended the camp, reflecting his commitment to familiarising himself with the various NCC units under his command and area of responsibility.

During his visit, he toured various areas of the campsite, including the firing simulator and the firing range, and interacted with the cadets.

The camp primarily seeks to familiarise NCC cadets with the challenges and discipline of camp life, while inculcating the fundamental values of unity, discipline, national integration and esprit de corps. The training curriculum has been designed to offer cadets a balanced blend of foundational military training and personality development activities. Activities including physical training, field craft and battle craft, small arms firing, musketry, weapons display, sword drill, lectures by distinguished personalities, yoga and games are incorporated into the training module. These activities are intended to build physical stamina, mental toughness, leadership abilities, teamwork, and self-confidence, thereby promoting the holistic development of every cadet.

In addition to the regular training programme, the camp will also feature training under the Yuva Aapda Mitra scheme, aimed at equipping NCC cadets with essential disaster preparedness and emergency response skills. The initiative, launched by the National Disaster Management Authority, seeks to develop volunteers capable of responding effectively during natural disasters and other emergencies. As part of the programme, cadets will undergo structured training in basic disaster response, relief and rescue operations, and emergency management techniques to handle situations such as floods, cloudbursts, and earthquakes.

The training is being imparted by certified instructors from the State Disaster Management Authority, enabling the cadets to acquire practical skills in disaster risk reduction and crisis response.

The camp has been planned to reinforce the NCC’s commitment to fostering discipline, leadership, physical fitness, and teamwork among the youths.

title: ‘GI Man of India’ visits NABARD RO
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/GI-Man-of-India-visits-NABARD-RO.webp

ITANAGAR, 27 Jun:
 Padma Shri awardee Dr Rajnikant, popularly known as the ‘GI Man of India’, visited the NABARD’s Arunachal Pradesh regional office (RO) here on Friday and held discussion with the officials on the NABARD’s interventions in the state for supporting rural artisans, weavers, craftsmen, farmers, FPOs, SHGs, etc.

NABARD GM SV Ranga Rao, AGM Vivek Anand and other officers of the NABARD were present during the discussion. Dr Rajnikant also held discussion on the NABARD’s role in supporting GI certification of various unique products of the state.

Initiatives for promoting and preserving GI products were discussed in detail, aiming to promote livelihood opportunities and help rural communities benefit from their rich cultural and natural heritage.

The NABARD has supported the highest number of GI sanctions and applications, and supported all types of products, including several ethnic and tribal products, said an official release.

Dr Rajnikant commended the proactive efforts of the NABARD to promote the state’s unique products. He emphasised the crucial role of GI tagging in preserving traditional knowledge, enhancing product value, and opening up better market opportunities for local communities.

The NABARD commended the role of Dr Rajnikanth and his agency in guiding and supporting the NABARD’s efforts in the state towards GI tagging of products, the release said.

title: Peddler  held with heroin in Deomali
content:
ITANAGAR, 27 Jun: A 39-year-old woman has been arrested with heroin valued at around Rs 4.5 lakh in Tirap district.

According to official sources, Nyamyang Thingnok was apprehended during a joint anti-drug operation conducted by the police and the local administration at Batey Colony in Deomali.

A police team carried out the raid at her residence, during which it seized the contraband along with Rs 9,700 cash, suspected to be sale proceeds from the drug trade, a mobile phone, and other incriminating materials.

According to the police, Thingnok had been on the run since 15 May this year, following the registration of a narcotics case and the subsequent arrest of two of her associates.

The accused had previously been arrested in another case under the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (NDPS) Act, the police said. (PTI)

title: Doyir Ete Taipodia’s ‘The Dance of the Last Leaf’
content:
image link: https://arunachaltimes.in/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Doyir-Ete-Taipodias-The-Dance-of-the-Last-Leaf-696x782.webp

TribalBookworms

[ Dr Bompi Riba ]

The Canadian indigenous poet, Frank Conibear in his poem ‘Artifacts’ questions if history can discover the whole truth through material evidences only.

He does not dismiss the importance of written records and archaeological findings, but he is of the opinion that the conventional historical approach often tends to overlook the lived community memories of the people in the present times as secondary evidence. He acknowledges that artefacts do reveal important aspects of the community history but they cannot completely reveal the belief system, emotions and values of the same. He highlights the paradoxical situation of how, despite the living memory of the people – preserved through oral narratives, traditions, cultural practices and personal recollections that sustains the existential meaning of a community’s past which is the source of its identity – the fixed records of archaeological artefacts and historical documents are often considered authentic proof of civilisation.

Doyir Ete Taipodia opens her debut collection, The Last Leaf with the poem ‘Lost Words’, which challenges this position by exposing how a tribal society is often seen as lacking by the literate culture which tends to question the authenticity of oral traditions and recognises only written records.

She begins by stating that “Our oralities are dismissed as hearsay,” mainly because of the lack of evidence in the form of a script. In the footnote to the poem, she explains that in Galo tribe, the written script is called Boi Bola. She provides a gist of the folk memory of how Abo Tani, the progenitor of the Tani tribes of Arunachal Pradesh, in a tragicomedy situation, eats the dried animal-skin, which consisted the knowledge of script, out of hunger or by mistake. According to the folk belief, the gift of script was given to Abo Tani by a benevolent spirit. She emphasises on how the indigenous communities, marked by the absence of their script, are forced to defend their history through their memories and stories.

From the western model of archaeological studies, the lack of script can be interpreted as no material evidence, but from the indigenous perspective, the fact that the story of the lost script exist is an evident of cultural artefact. This poem raises a pertinent question that does not ask as to where the script is but why the story about the script has survived. Like Conibear, Doyir Ete highlights the significant role played by the elders and shamans in forwarding the cultural knowledge across generations. It is to be noted that the poem does not really attempt to prove the physical existence of the script, but it draws attention towards the cultural anxiety created by the marginalisation and dismissal of indigenous knowledge due to the absence of the same. The poem evidently reflects on the two types of preservation, viz, the scribal culture of the historians and the oral culture of the community, and emphasises on how the latter is evidence of the resilience of indigenous memory and epistemology.

The title poem, ‘Last Leaf’ is a reflective appropriation of O Henry’s popular story but can be reimagined within the narrative of indigenous framework. On the surface level, it seems like a metaphorical meditation on loss, emotional devastation and the fragility of human relationship. But it makes a compelling reading when it is situated within the contemporary socio-political context marked by the division because of the ongoing debate surrounding the APFRA (Arunachal Pradesh Freedom of Religious Act) in Arunachal.

In popular sentiments, usually trees are used as symbols for a community’s connection to its roots. The expression used for the roots to describe the emotional state of the speaker represents a sense of pain where her death can also be read as a metaphor for the numbness that comes with the severing of the sense of belonging, cultural intimacy and the resultant loss of memory. The roots may also symbolise contemporary indigenous youths who are alive and yet deprived. It is in this line – “You survived but could not thrive on a foreign soil, uprooted by a gust of ill-timed prayer, like a promise that lovers make so untrue, yet so tender ” – that she voices her angst regarding the condition of the roots that are parched as a result of the absence of the native soil, which represents home and culture.

The “foreign soil” can literally be related to the act of migration or it can be understood metaphorically, as an act of assimilating into dominant cultures. The phrase “ill-timed prayer” is in fact interesting for it suggests, along with multiple interpretations, the possibility of the gradual rupture in the society that was brought apparently by forces that appeared benevolent or necessary like modernisation, education or conversion. Through this poem, she laments the consequential alienation from the ancestral ways of living through which men shared common kinship with nature. She might not have outrightly condemned these changes, some of which must have also been inevitable, but she acknowledges their sincerity and complexity at the same time.

Subsequently, the poet voices her anxiety as she witnesses the transformation of her society in the poem, ‘Crossroad’, where she addresses how generations of inherited culture have been relinquished by her people for Sunday masses and prayer beads. She attempts to negotiate the tension between indigenous belief system and Christianity in her ‘Native Tongue’. She does not simplify this difference in plain binaries. One can also notice that she does not outrightly criticise Christianity, as mentioned above, but she highlights the paradox of conversion, which according to her, is creating a new kind of a community that displaces the ancestral traditions. She also uses the indigenous terms like nyibo, dere, Tango, Jesse Kore, Kipu, and bipuin the poem with the intention of projecting the idea of how native tongues are repositories of memory and cultural continuity. While the message in the poem is loud and clear, it seems to give away its lyrical quality occasionally to cultural documentation. Maybe this is intentional on the part of the poet, but this hinders the realisation of a sustained poetic experience.

Another poem with the same thematic concern is ‘Past’, where the poet states bluntly that before the fraction was caused in her community by Christianity, her people shared the same origin. She alludes to the indigenous cosmology, according to which everything has originated from a cosmic womb. This cultural vision is powerful as it suggests that everyone was united until Christian concepts and binaries like ‘heaven-hell’, ‘blessed-condemned’, ‘believers-non-believers’, and ‘blessings-curses’ broke the primordial unity. Thus, the poem reflects on the cultural conflict that our state is facing now.

There are many interesting poems in this collection,such as ‘The Youngest’, based on a Galo folktale about a conjugal relationship between a girl and a python; ‘Our Journey’, that talks about ‘Golo-Yorbo’, the ancestral place of origin according to the Galos and ‘Gomku’, which is an archaic language used only by the priests. While most poems in the collection, such as ‘Yearning’, ‘Shaman’, ‘Ai Agam’, ‘Mopin’, ‘Amin’, and ‘Igi’ describe the traditional and cultural practices of the Galo tribe, viz, marriage, shamanism, festival, food habit and the tribal way of life; the poem ‘December’ localises Christianity in the City of Bricks, viz, Itanagar. It is to be noted that the approach of the poet in most poems set in contemporary times is observational. This poem is also not different and there are lots of visual images like ‘gold stars’ and ‘twinkling faith’ that serve both as religious symbols and the economic status of the houses of the Christian people living in Itanagar.

The poet observes that faith seems to transcend economic inequality in the capital city as Christmasstars hang brightly even from the roof of humble houses too. She also acknowledges that Christianity is no longer foreign to the natives as the procession of the locals singing carol represents how this religion is not exactly replacing but inhabiting the indigenous space. Rainer Maria Rilke in his ‘Letters to a Young Poet’ explains that a poet’s imagination can find art even in the most ordinary experiences. The poem ‘December’ is basically an ordinary observation but it succeeds in transforming a familiar moment into an evocative reflection on identity and cultural change.

Apart from poems dealing with cultural conflicts as the main subject, there are interesting poems on diverse subjects, such as ‘Squirrel’ that reflects on cultural tension caused by the tradition of hunting among the tribals despite the legal bans. This poem is elegiac and it laments for the vanishing wildlife. It is actually a beautiful recounting of an ephemeral encounter that makes the poet ponder over the subject of irreversible loss with the death of the Tibetan flying squirrel. One of my favourites in the collection is ‘Lady of the Meat Stall’, which presents the harsh working condition of a meat-seller, with a powerful image of her chopping meat while breast-feeding her baby at the same time. This image suggests the survival, resilience and tough life of a working class woman. The emotional potentiality of this poem is very high, but it remains unrealised mainly because of the poet’s position as an observer who predominantly records moments and that prevents her from being deeply reflective. However, it is also to be noted that the choice of words like ‘tousled stall’ for an ‘untidy stall’ and expressions like ‘prodding like scalpels’ to describe the staring eyes of the butcheress present the different positions of the two women. The poet observes from a position of privilege and the meat-seller becomes an object of emphatic speculation as she remains a passive character without any discernible voice.

Another poem worth mentioning is ‘City of Bricks’, where the poet, instead of monuments, statues or official buildings, opts for everyday eateries as contemporary landmarks of the capital city, Itanagar. This approach represents a people-centred view of the city. The poem contrasts between the new eateries, representing changing social patterns against the dead-old clock-tower and the squalid market area. Usually, a clock-tower stands as a heartbeat of the town, but in this case, it represents lost relevance of the temporal rhythm of the town. Ironically, the tower that the poem is referring to has been non-functional since the day of its inception. Isn’t it interesting? Then, there is a poem, ‘Old Villages’, the title of which is deceptively simple but the content is actually powerful. It talks about migration, depopulation, modernisation and the changes that are happening in the villages. The images of the abandoned jhum fields, baskets of homegrown produce, an unopened mobile phone, and the lingering routines of the old represent the discontinuity of intergenerational transition.

In conclusion, it would be right to claim that Doyir Ete Taipodia’s The Dance of the Last Leaf is an important contribution to Arunachali poetry. Her poems explore varied themes that include indigenous cosmology, memory, religious transformation and everyday life. Her voice is distinctive mainly because of her commitment to preserving and presenting the lived experiences of her people, but sometimes that tendency to document competes with the lyrical aspects of poetry, thereby hindering the realisation of the poems’ full poetic potentialities. (Dr Bompi Riba is an assistant professor in the English department of RGU. She is also a member of the APLS and Din Din Club)

title: From Lumla to California: A daughter of Arunachal and her family of books
content:
[ Prachi Sharma ]

I was born and raised among the hills of Arunachal Pradesh, in the small town of Lumla – and whatever I have become, it began there.

My father, Satya Prakash Sharma, came from a modest village and gave his working life to Arunachal as a senior government teacher, in a remote corner of the state where good teachers are everything. Later, when we children had grown, my mother, Manju Sharma, taught here too. Ours was a home of educators – and so, naturally, a home of books. They were never just objects on a shelf; they were how our family understood the world, and how we passed ourselves on.

The schools of Arunachal shaped me in ways I still carry. I studied hard in those classrooms, and I was fortunate to become a Class 10 state topper – an achievement that belongs as much to the teachers and the place that raised me as it does to me.

That love of books became a family inheritance. My mother preserved our family’s old, unwritten vegetarian recipes in her book The Lost Recipes, saving a generation’s flavours before they could be lost. I went on to write Manju, a tribute to her, and a children’s book, Paws around the World, featuring our rescue dog Oreo. My sister Richa wrote Finding Home Again. And the next generation didn’t wait – my son published two picture books at the age of six, and even read his book aloud at a public library; my nephew wrote a superhero story at eight.

Three generations. Seven books. One family – rooted in a childhood in Arunachal.

Today I live in California, where I run The Sapling Press, helping other children become the published authors of their own stories – carrying forward exactly what my teacher-parents gave me in Arunachal: the belief that every child has a story worth telling. Recently, India Currents, a respected Indian-American magazine in the US, featured our family’s journey.

Sometimes I think of those classrooms in Lumla, and of my parents teaching in a far-flung corner of the country, and I realise the whole journey began there – in the state that gave a young girl her foundation, and then watched her carry it across the world.

Arunachal made me. Wherever the books travel, that is where they started. 
(The writer is the founder of The Sapling Press, and can be contacted at 
[email protected]
)
